title: Scheduler Latency and Run Queue Depth
spl:
index=os sourcetype=vmstat host=*
| eval runq_to_cpu=r/procs_cpu
| stats avg(runq_to_cpu) as avg_ratio by host
| where avg_ratio > 2
description: High run queue depth with elevated scheduling latency causes visible application performance degradation.
value: High run queue depth with elevated scheduling latency causes visible application performance degradation.
implementation: Monitor run queue (r) field from vmstat and correlate with process count. When run queue exceeds 2x CPU count, alert on scheduler saturation. Create SPL to identify top CPU-consuming processes during high latency periods.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=vmstat, top, custom:sched_latency`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Monitor run queue (r) field from vmstat and correlate with process count. When run queue exceeds 2x CPU count, alert on scheduler saturation. Create SPL to identify top CPU-consuming processes during high latency periods.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=vmstat host=*
| eval runq_to_cpu=r/procs_cpu
| stats avg(runq_to_cpu) as avg_ratio by host
| where avg_ratio > 2
```

Understanding this SPL

**Scheduler Latency and Run Queue Depth** ‚Äî High run queue depth with elevated scheduling latency causes visible application performance degradation.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=vmstat, top, custom:sched_latency`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: vmstat. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=vmstat. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **runq_to_cpu** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where avg_ratio > 2` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
On the host, compare with `top`, `htop`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, or `sar` as appropriate to this use case. For log-only detections, compare with the relevant file under `/var/log` (or `journalctl`) on a test host. Confirm that indexed event counts and field values line up with what you see on the system and that your role can search the right indexes and fields.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timechart, Multi-series Line Chart
grandmaExplanation: High run queue depth with elevated scheduling latency causes visible application performance degradation ‚Äî so you find out before users do when something is slowing down or breaking.
knownFalsePositives: Boot, batch peaks, and wrong `procs_cpu` in your eval can inflate the ratio. Fix inventory fields before changing scheduler alerts.
